items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is the difference between gross profit and net profit?
items.correct: Gross profit = revenue minus cost of goods sold (direct costs only); net profit = revenue minus ALL costs including overheads, interest and tax. Net profit is the bottom line.
items.misconception: Gross profit and net profit are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Gross profit is profit before VAT and net profit is profit after VAT.
items.why: The gap between gross and net reveals overhead burden. A high gross but low net margin means heavy fixed costs or financing. Students conflate the two and misread profitability.

items.correct: Opening balance plus net cash flow
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Opening balance plus net cash flow. “Inflows minus outflows” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A profitable firm can still fail because of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Poor cash flow management. “Strong sales growth” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Opening balance 500, inflows 800, outflows 700. Closing balance?
items.why: Exact answer: 600. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
